title: ADaPT: Adaptive-window Decoding for Practical fault-Tolerance
authors: Tina Oberoi, Joshua Viszlai, Frederic T. Chong
abstract: Window decoding, first proposed to reduce decoding complexity for real-time decoding, is an essential component to realize scalable, universal-fault tolerant computation. Prior work has focused on improving throughput through parallelization and reducing reaction time via speculation on window boundaries. However, these methods use a fixed window size d, paying a fixed decoding time overhead for each window. In practice, we find this overhead of a fixed window size unnecessary in many cases due to the sparsity of average-case errors in QEC. Leveraging this insight, in this paper we propose an adaptive window decoding technique based on decoder confidence. This technique reduces the overhead in decoding time thus reducing reaction time without compromising on logical error rates. We benchmark adaptive window decoding across different codes and hardware inspired noise models. Our results show that this adaptive technique reaches the target error rate while maintaining a low decoding time overhead across different codes, and under different noise models.
summary: This paper proposes ADaPT, an adaptive-window decoding technique that reduces the computational latency of quantum error correction. By dynamically adjusting the decoding window based on decoder confidence, it maintains high accuracy while significantly lowering the decoding time overhead. This is crucial for achieving the real-time processing speeds required for scalable, fault-tolerant quantum computing.
main_problem: The high computational latency and decoding overhead caused by using fixed-size windows in real-time quantum error correction (QEC) decoding.
main_result: The ADaPT technique achieves target logical error rates while reducing average decoding time overhead to 0.4-0.6x of the standard fixed-window approach.
method: An adaptive-window decoding algorithm that uses a confidence metric derived from decoder soft information to expand the window size only when necessary, paired with a dynamic hypertuner to maintain a target retry rate.
model_or_system: The study evaluates performance on Toric codes and Bivariate Bicycle (BB) QLDPC codes under various noise models, including depolarizing noise and hardware-inspired models for neutral atoms and superconducting qubits.
key_observables: Logical Error Rate (LER), decoding time/latency, decoder confidence score (Q), and window retry rate (r_obs).
important_parameters: Code distance (d), window size (W), commit size (C), and physical error rate (p).
main_assumptions: The method assumes the availability of cluster statistics/soft information from the decoder and that the hypertuner can adjust thresholds based on observed retry rates.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the ADaPT workflow; Figure 2 shows super-linear scaling of decoding time with window size; Figure 3 shows error cluster distances; Figure 4 shows the correlation between confidence and LER; Figure 6 and 8 demonstrate LER and latency performance across different codes and noise models.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of decoding latency, proposes the ADaPT algorithm and confidence metric, describes the dynamic hypertuner control law, presents benchmarking results on Toric and BB codes under various noise models, and concludes with future integration opportunities.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: An overview of Adaptive Window Decoding Decod- ing starts with a small window d′ to obtain logical correction and soft information. A confidence score is computed; if confidence is high we commit correction C and proceed to the next window, otherwise the window is enlarged to d and retried. Concurrently, the observed retry rate robs is tracked to dynamically adjust Qthreshold. (Bottom right) Decoding with a reduced window size (d′ < d). The reaction time with d′

Fig. 2: Decoding time decreases superlinearly with smaller window sizes. The decoding time for toric codes with distance d = 7 using the BP+LSD decoder under depolarizing noise. The y-axis shows the decoding time for a window of size W, normalized by decoding time when W = d. The x-axis shows the window size W. Decoding time decreases superlinearly as W is reduced.

Fig. 3: Detector separation statistics in fixed window decoding of the d = 7 toric code. Window decoding with W = d is performed on the toric code under depolarizing noise for different p. Over all shots and windows, we record the maximum nearest-neighbour distance among triggered detectors — measured in space (2D toric Manhattan, top) and in time (|∆t|, bottom). In ≈90% of cases this maximum is ≤d/2, yet a fixed-W decoder incurs the full overhead of W = d in every window.

Fig. 4: Correlation of logical error rate (LER) with cluster- based confidence metric (Q). This is a memory experiment on the toric code under depolarizing noise with physical error rate p = 0.005, decoded using the BP-LSD. Here Q is calculated globally over the whole experiment. The plot shows that the logical error probability increases as Qllr increases, demonstrating the inverse dependence of LER on Q.

Fig. 5: Effect of logical error rate on changing commit size This figure shows that the logical error rate (LER) remains essentially unchanged for commit sizes c ∈{1, 2, 3} when the buffer size is fixed at d −1, for sliding-window decoding of the toric code at distance d = 7.
